entry.title: Against The Right, Brooks
entry.content:
currently writing


\







write your thoughts:

entry.title: The Delusion of Green Capitalism
entry.content:
[The following is the transcript of a part of the lecture by Slavoj Žižekat CUNY (New York) found here. Unfortunately the whole speech, which used to be here, is no longer available.]



So, there is, I claim, something deceptively reassuring in our readiness to assume full guilt and responsibility for the threats to our environment. We like to be guilty since if we are guilty it all depends on us, we pull the strings, so in principle we can also save ourselves by simply changing our lives. What is really difficult for us to accept is that we are reduced to the purely passive role of an impotent observer who can only sit back and watch what his fate will be. To avoid such a situation, we are prone to engage in frantic, obsessive activities- recycling paper, buying organic food, or whatever, just so that we can be sure we are doing something, making contribution[s]. Like a soccer or baseball fan who supports his team in front of a TV screen at home, shouting and jumping up from his seat, in the superstitious belief that this will somehow influence the outcome.



It is true that the typical form of fetishistic disavowal à propos ecology is that ‘I know very well that we are all dead, but I don’t really believe it, so I’m not ready to do anything really radical and change my way of life’. But I claim there is also the opposite form of disavowal, something like, ‘I know very well that I cannot really influence the process which may lead to my ruin, an earthquake or whatever. But it is nonetheless too traumatic for me to accept this, so I cannot resist the urge to do something even if I know it is ultimately meaningless.’ Do we not buy organic food for much the same reason? Who really believes that those semi-putrefying and overpriced apples are really healthier? The point is that by buying them we are not just buying and consuming a product, we are simultaneously doing something meaningful, demonstrating our capacity for care and global awareness, participating in a noble, large, collective project.



And I think (I don’t have time to develop it) that there is something of this same order which is no longer just a marginal phenomenon, but is more and more assuming a central role in how today’s capitalism functions. To me, the best example (I’m sorry if some of you know this, nonetheless I think it is important to repeat it) would be, what I call, the Starbucks logic. You know what you encounter when you go to a Starbucks coffee shop- basically the message is “true, our coffee is a little bit more expensive, but one cent from every coffee goes to Guatemala children, 5 cents go for water, blah blah blah”, in other words, the logic is the following one, and in a perverse way I like it. The logic is, in the old days, we were consumerists, and then we felt bad, and if you wanted to pretend to be an ethical being, you had to do something to counteract it. But the offer here is, we make it simpler for you, we make the product, you can remain just a consumerist, because your altruistic nature, solidarity with poor, it’s included into the price (you know).







write to me if you can find the whole lecture:
entry.summary: [The following is the transcript of a part of the lecture by Slavoj Žižekat CUNY (New York) found here. Unfortunately the whole speech, which used to be here, is no longer available.] So, there is, I claim, something deceptively reassuring in our readiness to assume full guilt and responsibility for the threats to our environment. We like to be guilty since if we are guilty it all depends on us, we pull the strings, so in principle we can also save ourselves by simply changing our lives. What is really difficult for us to accept is that we are reduced to the purely passive role of an impotent observer who can only sit back and watch what his fate will be. To avoid such a situation, we are prone to engage in frantic, obsessive activities- recycling paper, buying organic food, or whatever, just so that we can be sure we are doing something, making contribution[s]. Like a soccer or baseball fan who supports his team in front of a TV screen at home, shouting and jumping up from his seat, in the superstitious belief that this will somehow influence the outcome.

entry.title: On Faith
entry.content:
A lot of thoughts and ideas have been circling in my head endlessly for the last few days, and only a slow Thursday and an empty office gave me permission to put them down on paper. There’s not much direction here or development for that matter, but that is probably symptomatic of the ideas themselves.



Lately, I’ve been thinking a lot about religion and the distinction between organized and non-organized forms of it. The inherited repulsion to religion owing to my immigrant existence across the fault lines of Bengal, has always pushed me further away from all forms of religious texts and consequently rendered my arguments against religion perfectly anecdotal, weak, and easily refutible. I don’t know what the Bible says about same-sex marriage, I can only show you how it has affected the world. Maybe this perception of mine of how religious texts or the world around it works finds its own counterpart in fanatically religious people; we both have no idea of what is in the book, but we confidently arm ourselves on different sides of it.



My atheist friends would strongly argue, that not every invention and imagery that has been conjured into existence by a power-seeker needs to be studied, verified, or debunked. The responsibility to be truthful lies with the storyteller, it would be quite an endless and tedious pursuit to verify every hallucination of every “patient” in every mental health institution. Although I do not exactly disagree with this point, a distinction must be made for the imagery that has survived (and quite ruthlessly) the test of a few centuries. When you look at the current Hindu nationalist government in India, this matter becomes clearer. The religious tinge is not subtle in their speeches and campaigns, they attack the most unprotected psychological part of the human brain, the fear of the unknown and the other, the existence of which the privileged section of the Indian left even refuses to accept. Two groups of people provoked by this fundamental fear and acting under the perceived threat of non-existence are preached to by the left- don’t be afraid, both of your gods do not exist. Was religion created by the state as a weapon, or was it created by the people, in this primitive need for self-preservation, that we currently do not provide an acceptable alternative for? But I digress…



A lot of my thoughts and arguments often boil down to the one simple question — are humans inherently good or bad? There’s one supplementary question of what it really means to be good or bad, so I may rephrase the question as, do humans necessarily act in self-interest, and only act in the interest of others when a visible chain of events connects it to theirs? I don’t have an answer. Even in my manufactured honesty as an author (of this “essay”), I cannot claim to be a “good” person.



But back to religion. Today morning I had a session with my therapist, and we discussed the current issues I’m facing at my workplace among other daily struggles in my life which I won’t bore you with here. But it made me think, about how I go about my life, and how my perceived rationality is in reality extremely irrational. I’ve noticed quite often, the optimism yet the cold rationality with which the “religious” people I know take on the world, one thing leads to the other, one action secures an event, if it doesn’t, we have to reconsider the next action. God will take care of me. Whereas for me, the world is a landmine of cruel probabilities, nothing guarantees anything, and nothing is assured, every day I play a lottery against the lawlessness of nature and hope for the best. Life is a highly stochastic environment with sparse rewards, and no underlying fixed model you can learn, nothing you can prepare yourself for. One day you’re awaiting a positive response on your job application, and the same morning some unrelated tragic event subsumes your life. Nothing guarantees anything, and you can’t rely on anything. Every event has the potential to ruin your day or life, and you’re armed with nothing as you step onto this chessboard every morning. The “rationality” we atheists take our pride in, is it in reality our extreme helplessness in the irrationality of life? Handing your faith to imagery, to this assurance of certainty, isn’t that a more rational approach to life, in this continuously changing stochastic paradigm of existence?







write your thoughts:
entry.summary: A lot of thoughts and ideas have been circling in my head endlessly for the last few days, and only a slow Thursday and an empty office gave me permission to put them down on paper. There’s not much direction here or development for that matter, but that is probably symptomatic of the ideas themselves.

entry.title: Be Real
entry.content:
“Guys, quick, I’ve to do my BeReal!” I heard, in a heavy French accent right behind my back. I was on a day trip to Cadaqués, in Catalunya, and our trip guide Albert insisted that we go the “traditional” way, on a cruise ship from Roses. It was a bright Saturday morning, clear skies, and the unforgiving June sun banished me to the front corner of the ship, right in the shadow of the Captain’s cabin. The air was clean, I leaned against the railing, perhaps too precariously, and watched the wide foam trail our ship left behind on the turquoise sea, the paddle boaters sitting down for a better balance as the waves from our ship raced towards the rocky shores. Two speedboats rushed past us, keenly aware of our eyes on them, along with a group of fishermen, although I suspect they were on more of a pleasure than a work affair.



Two French girls, rushed past me to the front of the ship, also possibly disturbed by the sun, but ended up choosing a spot right in front of me. The one right next to me was in a tight black dress, spoke fluently in French to the other, and occasionally broke into songs in her language. The gust blew her auburn curly hair into my face a few times, but I didn’t complain and continued to fiddle with the camera in my hand. The views of the barren rocky shore grew monotonous, and halfway through our journey, I found myself distracted by the occasional English they spoke. They started out taking some pictures for the “gram”, posing with themselves hanging halfway outside the railing. Just as I was praying that the support doesn’t give away, a third girl ran up to them and exclaimed, “Guys, quick, I’ve to do my BeReal!”, and they posed together right behind me, and I found myself questioning if I should move or not, which would give away the gross intrusion of privacy I was engaged in, so I stayed.



BeReal. It wasn’t popular in India when it came about, and maybe it still isn’t now, but I heard about it while researching the effects on mental health from social media, especially Instagram. While Insta offered you an endless supply of self-hate fuel by methodically exposing you to lifestyle envy, BeReal promised a unique respite. For those who don’t know, the app prompts you to take a picture of your day at a random time, both with a front and back camera, to show what you’re really doing, the goal being taking away the pretense from social media. But does this really assure any kind of authenticity? Does it really provide relief from this strategic and banal self-representation we engage in every day? As a 25 years old grad student, social media is the online equivalent of hard drugs for my self-esteem so I did not dare to sign up just to give it “one try”, but I found myself contemplating throughout the trip, what it means to be ready, ready to perform an act of self-expression. “Look at the sea, this is my best BeReal ever”, she exclaimed to her friend, who I suspect nodded in agreement. Does BeReal show you the real life of your acquaintances, or is it a more calibrated way of letting your friends know you’re interesting, even when you’re unprepared, even when its not choreographed, almost lending some authenticity to the edited, planned, and timed Instagram posts that’ll inadvertently follow? The exhibition of a truly effortless, seductive, and therefore more enviable lifestyle, once a day, every day.



I took a few more pictures, as the ship banked towards Cadaqués, the small white buildings with their beige roofs almost blinding in the sun against the dark still sea. The otherwise quiet town was littered with tourists, bars, and quaint shops selling everything from Indian beach wear, to Indian hippie hemph essentials. Young children ran across the narrow uphill streets ornate with overgrown bougainvilleas, squealing and shouting in languages I’m sure only they understood. We spent the first hour guided by a French historian, Myriam, who walked us, in broken English, through the churches and folk tales surrounding Dali and Gala, and etymologies of Hebrew and Catalan words, which my boyfriend later assured were not entirely correct. I believe it was lost in the labored French-english translation Myriam wasn’t expecting to do that day.



A few hours and a perfectly cooked beef tenderloin later, I found myself standing against one of the bright white restaurant walls, facing the sea, trying to make a quick sketch of this picturesque frame which bore a stronger resemblance to Santorini than Catalunya. The bustling crowd behind me grew slower and quieter, as the day moved on, and I was pleasantly interrupted by a Cuban photographer who politely asked if I could pose for a few pictures, and I obliged. I wrapped up my sketch and called my friend who was sunbathing on the beach, and we went to get some dessert. Understandably, as it was close to 4 pm, the entire state of Catalonia was on their third nap of the day, except for one ice cream shop, with a queue of parents and sea-drenched children covered in sand waiting outside. We got our ice creams and started to look for our ride home to Barcelona, and just as I was walking past the beach, my eyes caught the two French girls in their swimsuits and a towel around their shoulders, sitting on opposite ends of a bench, deeply immersed in their phones.



The sun was still bright, I could feel the eyes of seagulls narrowing in on the cup of mango ice cream I held tightly in my hand. We turned around to take one last look at the shores of Cadaqués before we start rushing towards the bus station. The still waters, the busy group of scuba teachers preparing a group of excited tourists for their first dive, an old couple setting up a picnic blanket on the sand like they’ve done a thousand times before, it was unprepared and banal; like living the same day again and again, the new faces that found themselves on the shore everyday merging into one another; it was so boring and expected that I had forgotten to take out my phone.







write your thoughts:
entry.summary: “Guys, quick, I’ve to do my BeReal!” I heard, in a heavy French accent right behind my back. I was on a day trip to Cadaqués, in Catalunya, and our trip guide Albert insisted that we go the “traditional” way, on a cruise ship from Roses. It was a bright Saturday morning, clear skies, and the unforgiving June sun banished me to the front corner of the ship, right in the shadow of the Captain’s cabin. The air was clean, I leaned against the railing, perhaps too precariously, and watched the wide foam trail our ship left behind on the turquoise sea, the paddle boaters sitting down for a better balance as the waves from our ship raced towards the rocky shores. Two speedboats rushed past us, keenly aware of our eyes on them, along with a group of fishermen, although I suspect they were on more of a pleasure than a work affair.

entry.title: The Case For Dark Humor
entry.content:
There’s something very profoundly surreal about watching our generation respond to an imminent WWIII in memes and satire, and though some might find it culturally insensitive and “very gen-z”, a look back in the pre-WWII era shows us that perhaps it is neither.



Back in 1916, in the aftermath of the first world war, poet Hugo Bell founded the Cabaret Voltaire in Switzerland, a club which set the stage for artistic expression in the form of sound poetry, cut-up writing and even sculpture. The overwhelming tone of the art here was anti-war and anti-establishment, and a very open rejection of the gatekeepers of art in the modern world, embracing irrationality, and illogicality.



The art movement which would soon be referred to as Dadaism, spread quickly across Europe, in Paris, Cologne, Netherlands, etc. It was characterized by art which can be called “silly” or “nonsensical” and quickly descended into absurdist humor. Marcel Duchamp’s famous work on a postcard with Da Vinci’s Monalisa, involved drawing a mustache on her face.



Marcel Duchamp, 1919, L.H.O.O.Q.1



The nonsensical art movement spread all across the world and became the birth place of what we know as Surrealism, and would see artistic contributions from non other than Salvador Dali, from his timeless still frames to his 1929 surrealist short film Andaluský pes2 (An Andalusian Dog), created by both Dali and Buñuel which contains surreal scenes of a man cutting an eye with a razor, a woman holding a severed human hand, a man’s hand infested with ant, all seemingly disconnected from each other. The movie rejected the traditional understanding of a plot, with disjoint chronology and a dream-like narrative.



Scene from Andaluský pes (An Andalusian Dog), 1929, a man holding a razor to a woman’s eye



Francis Picabia, another great artist propagating the movement, created the Tableau Rastadada in 1920, where he created a collage of himself with cutouts from various magazines, to depict himself as a casanova.



Francis Picabia, Tableau Rastadada, 1920



The reason for the rise of this movement is highly debated, although a crucial motivating factor might have been the helplessness of artists and the youth in the face of a war-torn Europe. The movement began on the graveyard of hopes after WWI, as a defiance of the institutions of art, and as a rejection of every rationality, embracing the absurd-ism of reality.



The creation of absurdist art using pre-made material, nonsensical messaging, surrounding an imminent war, draws a natural parallel to today’s dark humor among the youth, in the face of life-changing crises in the form of war, climate change, pandemic, and political unrest all around the world. The constant accessibility of negative news from all corners, the live streaming of air-strike sirens from the capital of a country being invaded, the pleas of help from citizens not alike to us, have only increased the feeling of deep inconsequentiality.



Perhaps what comes off as ‘insensitive’ to certain viewers, is a desperate attempt from the young to hold on to hope, maybe embracing absurdism in the face of a cruel, unforgiving reality, is the only way to survive through it. Perhaps the realisation of this inconsequentiality of our actions, our protests, and our petitions can only be dealt with that, humor.



write your thoughts:






references:

  1. Marcel Duchamp 1887–1968, dadart.com (here). ↩

  2. The video used to be here, but has since been removed. ↩
entry.summary: There’s something very profoundly surreal about watching our generation respond to an imminent WWIII in memes and satire, and though some might find it culturally insensitive and “very gen-z”, a look back in the pre-WWII era shows us that perhaps it is neither.

entry.title: Metoo And Cancel Culture
entry.content:
[note: This contains my outdated take on cancel culture, written by a young(er) and (more) immature and violent version of me in the wake of the “me-too” movement in the upper social classes of Jadavpur University (JU), my alma mater. The arguments for this half-baked post were borrowed from this brilliant video by Natalie Wynn. The original blog was published in 2020, with a note claiming to be a concise summary of said video, and I have no doubt I did not succeed. Please watch the video instead, this blog is here for documentation purposes. Thank you.]

I understand the risks of being misinterpreted when approaching a topic so ill-debated and nuanced, I urge you to PLEASE reach out to me, CORRECT me where I’m wrong, and ENGAGE in a debate. I’m open to every varying opinion from anyone. Consider all possible trigger warnings, ask me to add more as you feel fit.



[I understand that presenting an anti-call-out argument risks it being immediately appropriated by some people to advance their bigoted agenda. If you don’t believe that women should have equal fundamental rights as men or some sugar-coated variation of this, I disagree with absolutely everything you have to say in this regard. We proceed with the understanding that we both want justice, a more equal space, and most importantly, we both are feminists.]



When the #metoo movement began, it began as a movement of gender rights, of justice, the ever echoing call to break down the hierarchy of gender and power. From R. Kelly to Harvey Weinstein, #metoo called to bring down men who could not be held accountable in any way other than vigilante justice. This part is important but let’s put this aside for a while now.



In JU, what we owe to #metoo and call-out culture is the environment that it has tried to create, to allow those abused to come forward and openly talk about the issues and problems in the campus. I doubt the Google Drive incident could’ve come out so openly and strongly if it weren’t for the waves of #metoo that had pushed the boundaries of our understanding of abuse, repeatedly. It taught us that rapists are not necessarily the stereotypical men who hide in bushes and hold you at gunpoint, they are people who walk among us, are even nice to us. We have actively dismissed the argument “but he never did this with me, so…”, and I can’t emphasise how important this was. There are lot more barriers to break, and a lot more for us to learn. I can go on, but the positives of this movement have been written and spoken about again and again (I’ll leave some links of my favorite books/articles down below if you want to read), but there are things which haven’t yet been brought to light as clearly. Gender discrimination is as old as time itself, but the internet is new, this form of the movement is new, therefore its criticism is also new. I’ll lean on my Mommy to explain this, so let’s get to the problems that are happening right now. 



There are essentially two types of popular arguments that you’ll find on the internet over this- one group claiming that the call-out culture has ruined intimacy and flirting, it has gone “too far”, men can’t even talk to women anymore, and something needs to be done to stop this nonsense. The other group of feminists and allies claiming that the movement is finally bringing accountability to powerful ‘abusers’ and your fear is your admission of guilt. Call me a centrist, but none of these truly grasp the nuance of gender discriminations and consequently the nuances of the movements associated with it.



When I started to write this down, I was scared of attacking certain beliefs and institutions, I was afraid of a backlash. But why?

 No movement has been spared criticism, so why do I feel so afraid of criticizing this one? That is precisely my first criticism. We’ve been throwing labels like ‘enabler’, ‘abuser’, ‘MRA’ so frivolously and recklessly, that the terms which carry much significance, lose its weight in case of serious offences. I feel afraid of being told that I’m seeking “male validation” while critiquing a movement that talks of the oppression I face. All of my criticism about the movement mainly focuses on the traps that I have myself fallen into, and I will admit, I have been blinded by the reactionary politics around this. Which brings me to my first point. 



ABSTRACTION

The term you’ve heard often is “enabler” and “abuser”. The allegations that these terms carry are severe and meaningful. If you take a look at the Google Drive case, the people who initiated it, and/or perpetuated the activities, undoubtedly deserve these labels. But are all the allegations of the same magnitude?



Let’s say a person asks someone to sext with them unprompted in 2017, and then gets blocked? Creepy right. I’ve been there on the other side of this. And to the women reading this, I’m sure this is not an uncommon experience for you too. The action of the person in question is undoubtedly abusive. But does this call for the person to be termed an “abuser”? I’ve seen posts like these calling out people as “toxic”, “abusive”, “predator”, and my first speculations about the specifics are always pretty bad. Are we absolutely sure that there is no space for reformation of this person, no space for education? Aren’t these blanket statements a bit too vague? Should these heavy labels be used indiscriminately on everyone? 



Another popular trope is “enabling”. Oh, how hard I had fallen for this myself. We have all sat with people who have made sexist jokes, said repulsive things about women, and no matter what image of yourself you project on your social media account, you too have slut-shamed someone or the other at some point in your life. And it’s really not your fault. We have been brought up in a system where slut-shaming, competing with, and demeaning other women for their femininity and choices are INCENTIVISED. This behavior has sedimented inside our brains for long, and we are all trying to unlearn it. Is it fair, to drag every single friend of the “accused” down the same hole as the accused themselves, without giving them a chance at retribution, self-reflection, and analysis? Are they(we) all really equally as guilty as the perpetrators of the Google-Drive case to receive the same disproportional response? What was the point of “calling out” these enablers ? Did they learn from their mistakes? Did they grow as people? Or did we just shove them into a hole of account-deactivation and self-isolation?



MOTIVE

The personal is political, so I cannot leave myself completely removed in the objective sideline. A lot of my friends have called out the people who have abused and exploited them, and since I’ve known them and their account on a very personal level, I can tell you their motivation to call-out was perfectly genuine. I’m sure you’ve had a similar experience as mine or my friends. 



But is that always the case? Let’s take a look at the fall of a very popular couple, (I do not personally know them) R & S. Absolute social butterflies, vocal intersectional feminists, whatnot. And apparent from the screenshots, had engaged in pretty fucked up activities. Now I’m no one to tell you whether they are capable of reparations or not. Maybe they’re repeat offenders, who knows. But take a closer look at the comment section. You cannot overlook the fact that a multitude of people who were slandering them, were not motivated by the slightest sense of moral integrity. It was about a fall, a spectacle - what initially appeared like the guillotine, was actually like one, a source of perverted and sadistic fascination. 



How many of us really want to see the improvement of these people? How many of us want an actual apology and move forward towards creating a more coherent narrative in this fight? I do not have the answers. You have to ask yourself.



SO WHAT DO WE REALLY WANT FROM THE ACCUSED?

Let’s say you (yes, YOU) have been called out as an ‘abuser’ or an ‘enabler’. I don’t know how sincere it is because I don’t know you, and this pitfall is a HUGE POINT OF CONCERN. No one knows. What can you do at this point?



Here are three things you can do, and it’ll still never be appropriate for the internet. 

a) Apologize- Once you apologize right away, with or without realizing what you did wrong, or even taking the time to understand if you did anything wrong at all, your apology will be tagged as insincere, and a “manipulative attempt” to coerce the victim into forgiving you.



b) Try to explain your position - oops, your timeline is now a dumpster fire. You will be accused of being incapable of self-introspection, of invalidating the trauma of the victim, when you didn’t even get a chance to explain your position publicly. 



c) Stay silent- now I totally wanted to make a graph of account deactivations in Kolkata in the past one week, but I’m lazy. If you deactivate or refuse to comment immediately, there can be a lot of reasons for that. Maybe you’re thinking of a proper way of response, or maybe you’re trying to understand exactly what went wrong. Whatever it may be, you’ll immediately be accused of fleeing accountability, and gaslighting the victim. 



We fall into this trap that an instant apology is more sincere than a delayed one. I know for a fact that I indulged in this delusion. It takes time to understand where you went wrong, to self-introspect, to get over the overwhelming nature of the accusations, and respond and improve yourself. It can take more than days. 



What we do by expecting immediate unconditional apologies is stifle an environment encouraging self-improvement, and this one is probably the most dangerous, we encourage insincere pointless apologies from people who in reality deserve no forgiveness for the heinousness of their crime. We snub people capable of change and forgive those incapable of such. 



Needlessly to say I absolutely believe that there are people who are so harmful to those around them, that they do not deserve a voice anywhere, let alone forgiveness. So what should we be doing about them? Which brings us to



JUSTICE

So that thing we put aside, about Harvey Weinstein? Let’s bring that back. What #metoo promised was to bring down men who were revered, above all criticism, and considered to be beyond law and accountability. #metoo wanted to bring justice to victims that were impossible to attain at least initially through any due process. Is that really the case here? Besides an obvious few accused, others simply do not have that social capital, public relation agents defending them, are not insanely rich and do not have contacts that we understand are imperative for social survival. So are we kicking people out of orbit without due process? People who we can conventionally bring to justice?



One problem that I have faced while discussing this is the problem of the statistics. “Most sexual abuse accounts are true”. Given the context of this country and our personal experiences, you really don’t need a number to make that comment. It is invariably true. Facebook is the tip of the iceberg when it comes to the struggle in the lives of women and non-binary folks. But from what I understand, #metoo is a movement of liberation, a fight for equality, a call for dismantling the power structure. This is not a war against people who are capable of change. This is not a war where we can isolate and abandon people without any due process, or any regards for their rights, and declare it is collateral damage for a greater cause. This is the greater cause. Changing people, reforming human beings, this is the greater cause. The current fight at least how I see it has shifted from this principle axis that it started with. 



If you ask yourself and the people around you how they think they can bring any solution to this, the most common answer that you’re likely to get is “sensitization”. We need to educate kids, teachers, and parents, about consent, bodily autonomy, sexual orientation, identity, gender roles, etc. We should be taking immediate efforts towards this, but I hate to admit it, it is a LONG process. Reformation is tedious, abuse is real and immediate. So how do we go about this?



As I see things now, no matter how much you want to abolish the State and establish the new world order, the law does have the capability to bring some sort of justice to those who are dangerous if left without intervention. Yes, the process is tedious, legal harassment is real. There are tonnes of violating questions and tests that a victim might have to go through before their innocence is even considered. Like the two-finger-test. I’m not gonna describe how brutally violating it is, but through the efforts of people like you and me, who are seeking justice, in 2013 in the case of Lilu @ Rajesh and anr v. State of Haryana, the two-finger-test was declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court (read here). Similarly, through the LGBTQIAA+ movements, and repeated protests and fights homosexuality was decriminalized as well (read here). Our fights for our rights are very very far from being over, and you can say that these are very small stepping stones in that journey, but I will still advocate that these steps are important. They have real-life and immediate implications. 



Social media is an outlet for our grievances with the system, the hierarchies, and the bureaucracies, but the fight beyond that remains imperative. When we can bring these perpetrators who are not really above law, to justice through the hands of law itself, wouldn’t we be creating a better narrative? A chance for everyone to avail of justice? Shouldn’t we be fighting tooth and nail against the laws that are rigged against us, against our humanity and rights to fair treatment? I can only appeal to you to fight and nothing more, I can only assure you that we will fight together.



WHY I DIDN’T CALL OUT 

This is the part where I need to clear out something very personal. I think that being able to open up about the abuse that you have faced or still do, it a very important step towards justice. What #metoo has done is it has opened to us the dimensions of abuse, and I learn more and more about it every day. Starting this discussion is a very brave thing to do, but I found it to be an utterly harassing experience to be so vulnerable so openly, just to demand justice that I really deserve. Just take a look at the posts of the victims who have opened up. Almost every single one of them face a tremendous amount of abuse and harassment after calling someone out, and the entire thing doesn’t even move a single step towards providing them justice. Why aren’t we fighting for a system where people can fight for their rights without having to bare their most traumatizing moments in front of the most heartless crowd on earth? How can a system so dehumanising for everyone involved, be a solution?



During the recent “discourse” over justice and social media trails, for reasons that I have been trying to explain so far, I have absolutely lost faith that I can avail of any form of justice through this tool. Social media shaming is a horrifying thing, which I don’t think I or the person in question deserves. It would be a permanent scar because remember, nothing disappears off the internet, the screenshots and accounts will keep circulating and it would end up being a traumatizing experience for both of us, without a single push towards self-improvement or actualization. I’d, of course, be slut-shamed by some, my narrative twisted and needlessly politicized and weaponized by others when all I wanted is for both of us to be better people, to create a healthy environment for discussion and understanding. 



While I believe that the actions of the person in question were truly wrong, it is still a huge leap from criticizing the person’s action to criticizing the person themselves, a leap I’m not willing to make. Whatever accusation I throw, irrespective of the severity of it, will be oversimplified and misappropriated by labeling them as “abuser”, “predator” and whatnot, with absolutely no acceptance for his side of the story. What if I have been emotionally abusive? That’s not far off. What if I have been manipulative? Even if you extend me immense trust that I am not, I can assure you no amount of honesty and humility will make me admit to my own mistakes as openly when I ax him for his. And why will I be worthy of this trust in the first place? What if I’m someone you don’t know? Or someone you don’t like? By now, this is a real possibility. How can I appeal for justice based on the trust I have “earned” through my social capital, how is that just for the person I’m accusing? How is that just for you, as a third party, to be pushed into accepting my account as gospel, and shun someone you barely know into a dark hole of rejection? 



THE PROBLEM IS BIGGER THAN THAT

Why I felt the need to say all of this is that the dumpster fire that Facebook is right now, what we are doing is actively escalating conflict instead of promoting any way to come to a mutual understanding and fight together for justice. We are creating respective echo chambers for feminists and men’s rights activists, and everyone who does not agree to our exact brand of feminism, we push them outside to fend for themselves. Someone who has his life thrown into disarray for a crime he didn’t even understand that he committed since we gave him no space for introspection and discussion, which echo chamber do you think he’ll find himself in? Is that what we want? 



I’m not misdirecting my anger, I’m not calling any feminism “imperfect” or “pseudo”. I’m trying to appeal to you, to reconsider and reevaluate what exactly we are fighting for, and why we have shifted. Needless to say, call-out culture is effective when it comes to men who are so powerful and harmful, that letting them speak is an assault to the idea of democracy itself. But in a temporary, reactionary attitude to reprimand these people, we’re potentially ruining lives which could’ve been bettered, and more importantly derailing a movement which is crucial and can effectively bring exactly criminals like these to justice. 



Movements are meant to be moulded through criticism and introspection. But it seems impossible now to challenge narratives and expect any respectful engagement, and that’s precisely what I want to change. 

For example, I find the phrase “Believe women” very misleading. At least how it’s used conventionally. In a country like India, where women are judged, slut-shamed, raped, blamed for it, and then shoved in a corner of disbelief, this phrase is very crucial. What this implies is that an overwhelming majority of violence against women is real, and a huge percentage of it is disbelieved simply because they are not men. Believe women means don’t disbelieve someone because they are women, it does NOT mean believe everyone who is a woman.



We have to understand, no matter how overwhelmingly true the accounts may be, “believe women” when set as a norm, will be very easy to abuse. 



You can tell me I’m wrong, correct me, and engage, I’m willing to learn, but how many of are up for that effort? There’s a particular amount of comfort in just labeling me an “enabler”, a “sympathizer”, etc, and just shut out everything I tried to challenge. The difficult part is self-introspection, to identify where we are going wrong, to question everything, EVERYTHING.



As you had promised you have passed me the mic. How does it matter if no one is willing to listen?

Important note - I have attempted to be as inclusive in my language as possible, if you notice any exclusion, it’s not deliberate, please correct me.
entry.summary: [note: This contains my outdated take on cancel culture, written by a young(er) and (more) immature and violent version of me in the wake of the “me-too” movement in the upper social classes of Jadavpur University (JU), my alma mater. The arguments for this half-baked post were borrowed from this brilliant video by Natalie Wynn. The original blog was published in 2020, with a note claiming to be a concise summary of said video, and I have no doubt I did not succeed. Please watch the video instead, this blog is here for documentation purposes. Thank you.]
